title: 10 best herbal teas for flu

remedies.remedy_desc: Elderberry tea helps with flu because it contains properties that can help to reduce symptoms and duration of the virus. The tea is rich in antioxidants, vitamins, and minerals, particularly vitamin C, that can help to boost the immune system and fight off the virus. Additionally, elderberries have been found to contain antiviral properties that can prevent the virus from replicating, thereby reducing the severity and duration of the flu. Furthermore, elderberry tea can help to alleviate symptoms such as fever, headache, and sore throat, providing relief and comfort to those suffering from the flu. However, it is important to note that while elderberry tea can help to manage flu symptoms, it is not a substitute for medical treatment, and individuals should consult with a healthcare professional for severe or persistent symptoms.
remedy_recipe: To make elderberry tea for the flu, start by boiling 1 cup of water in a saucepan, then add 1-2 tablespoons of dried elderberries (or 2-3 tablespoons of fresh elderberries) and let it simmer on low heat for 15-20 minutes.
Strain the tea into a cup, using a fine-mesh strainer or cheesecloth, to remove the elderberries.
Optional: To enhance the tea's medicinal properties and soothe a sore throat, add 1-2 slices of fresh ginger, 1 cinnamon stick, or a few whole cloves during the simmering process (step 1).
For added sweetness and vitamin C, squeeze half a lemon and stir in 1-2 teaspoons of raw honey after straining the tea.
Drink the tea while it's still warm, 2-3 times a day, as needed, for relief of flu symptoms.

remedies.remedy_desc: Echinacea tea helps with flu because it contains properties that can boost the immune system, potentially reducing the duration and severity of flu symptoms. The tea is made from the leaves and flowers of the echinacea plant, which are rich in antioxidants and have anti-inflammatory effects. Some studies suggest that echinacea may help to reduce inflammation in the respiratory system, alleviating congestion and coughing. Additionally, echinacea may have antiviral properties, which could help to prevent the flu virus from replicating in the body. However, it's important to note that echinacea should not be used as a substitute for medical treatment for the flu, and individuals with certain medical conditions or who are taking certain medications should consult with a healthcare provider before using echinacea.
remedy_recipe: To make echinacea tea for the flu, start by boiling 2 cups of water and adding 1-2 teaspoons of dried echinacea flowers and/or leaves, then let it simmer for 10-15 minutes.
Optional: add other flu-fighting ingredients such as 1 tablespoon of freshly grated ginger, 1 cinnamon stick, and/or a few cloves for added flavor and immune-boosting properties.
After simmering, remove the tea from heat and let it cool for a few minutes before straining out the solid plant material.
Stir in a natural sweetener like raw honey (to taste, usually 1-2 teaspoons) for added soothing effects on a sore throat.
Sip the tea while still warm, 2-3 times a day, to help alleviate flu symptoms and support the immune system in fighting off the virus.

remedies.remedy_desc: Peppermint tea helps with flu because it contains menthol, a compound with natural decongestant and expectorant properties that help clear nasal congestion and soothe a sore throat. Its antiviral and antimicrobial effects can potentially reduce flu symptoms and shorten the duration of the illness. The warm temperature of the tea can help alleviate body aches and pains, while its hydrating properties can prevent dehydration caused by fever and excessive sweating. Additionally, peppermint tea can help stimulate digestion and improve nutrient absorption, which is crucial for boosting the immune system and promoting faster recovery.
remedy_recipe: In a teapot, combine 1 teaspoon of dried peppermint leaves or 2 peppermint tea bags with 8 ounces of boiling water, then let it steep for 5-10 minutes to allow the flavors and therapeutic oils to infuse.
While the tea is steeping, crush 1 peppermint candy or peppermint lozenge using a mortar and pestle or the back of a spoon, creating small, fine pieces.
After the steeping time has elapsed, remove the peppermint leaves or tea bags and stir in the crushed peppermint candy until fully dissolved.
Optional: For added vitamin C and soothing qualities, squeeze half a lemon's juice into the tea and add a small dollop of honey (about 1/2 to 1 teaspoon) to taste.
Serve the peppermint tea warm, sipping it slowly to soothe a sore throat and relieve congestion, and enjoy the calming effects to help you rest and recover from the flu.

remedies.remedy_desc: Ginger tea helps with flu because it contains antiviral and anti-inflammatory properties that can help the body fight off the flu virus and alleviate symptoms. The gingerol and shaogals in ginger tea can inhibit the growth of harmful bacteria and reduce inflammation in the respiratory tract. Additionally, ginger tea can help soothe a sore throat, relieve congestion, and boost the immune system due to its high antioxidant content. Furthermore, ginger tea can also help alleviate nausea and vomiting, common symptoms associated with the flu. Overall, ginger tea's natural anti-inflammatory, antiviral, and immune-boosting properties make it an effective natural remedy for treating flu symptoms.
remedy_recipe: Peel and thinly slice 1 inch of fresh ginger, then combine in a pot with 4 cups of water, 1 cinnamon stick, and 1 tablespoon of honey; bring to a boil.
Reduce heat to low, cover and let it simmer for 10 minutes to allow the flavors to meld together.
Remove from heat, add the juice of half a lemon and optional 1/4 teaspoon of turmeric; stir well.
Strain the tea through a fine-mesh sieve into a cup, pressing on the solids to extract all liquid.
Serve warm and enjoy this soothing ginger tea, perfect for helping to alleviate flu symptoms.

remedies.remedy_desc: Licorice root tea helps with flu because it has antiviral and anti-inflammatory properties that can reduce symptoms and duration of the flu. The active compound in licorice root, glycyrrhizin, inhibits the replication of the flu virus and boosts the immune system's response to the virus. Additionally, licorice root tea can soothe a sore throat, relieve coughing, and alleviate congestion, making it easier to breathe. Furthermore, it can help break up phlegm and mucus, making it easier to expel them from the body. However, it is important to note that licorice root tea should be consumed in moderation, as excessive consumption can lead to side effects such as high blood pressure and hormonal imbalances.
remedy_recipe: To make licorice root tea for flu, bring 4 cups of water to a boil in a saucepan, then add 1-2 tablespoons of dried licorice root and let it simmer for 15-20 minutes.
Strain the tea into a teapot or a heat-resistant container, discarding the used licorice root.
Optionally, add honey or lemon to taste for added flavor and potential additional flu-fighting benefits.
Pour the tea into a cup, ensuring it's warm and comfortable to drink.
Sip the tea slowly, allowing it to soothe your throat and potentially alleviate some flu symptoms.

remedies.remedy_desc: Chamomile tea helps with flu because it has anti-inflammatory and analgesic properties that can alleviate symptoms such as fever, headache, and muscle aches. Its soothing effect on the digestive system can help reduce nausea and vomiting, common in flu cases. Chamomile tea is also a natural antioxidant and contains antimicrobial properties that can help boost the immune system and fight off the flu virus. Furthermore, its calming effects can promote better sleep, which is essential for the body to recover from the flu. However, it should be noted that while chamomile tea can help manage flu symptoms, it is not a cure and should not replace medical treatment.
remedy_recipe: In a cup, pour boiling water (8 ounces) over 2 teaspoons of dried chamomile flowers, cover and let it steep for 15 minutes.
Strain the tea to remove the chamomile flowers and discard them.
Add honey (1-2 teaspoons) or lemon juice (1-2 teaspoons) to taste, if desired.
Warm the tea in the microwave or on the stove if it has cooled down, to a comfortable drinking temperature.
Sip the tea slowly, relaxing and allowing the chamomile's soothing properties to help with flu symptoms.

remedies.remedy_desc: Cinnamon tea helps with flu because it has anti-inflammatory, antiviral, and antioxidant properties that can help alleviate symptoms and strengthen the immune system. The warm temperature of the tea can soothe a sore throat, while the natural oils in cinnamon, such as cinnamaldehyde, can help fight off viruses and bacteria. Additionally, cinnamon tea can help reduce congestion and promote respiratory health by acting as an expectorant, helping to clear mucus and phlegm from the respiratory tract. Furthermore, cinnamon tea can help boost energy levels and reduce fatigue, which are common symptoms of the flu. Lastly, cinnamon tea is a natural and caffeine-free alternative to other beverages, making it a healthy and comforting choice for those looking to soothe flu symptoms.
remedy_recipe: In a pot, bring 1 cup of water to a boil, then add 1 cinnamon stick and 1 teaspoon of honey, reducing heat to a simmer for 10 minutes.
Crush 1/2 teaspoon of cloves and add to the pot, simmering for an additional 3 minutes.
Remove from heat, then add 1 teaspoon of lemon juice and optional 1/2 teaspoon of grated ginger; cover and steep for 5 minutes.
Strain the tea into a cup, discarding the solids.
Serve hot, sip slowly, and repeat as needed for relief of flu symptoms.

remedies.remedy_desc: Yarrow tea helps with flu because it contains anti-viral and anti-inflammatory properties that can reduce symptoms and shorten the duration of the illness. The tea's natural chemicals, such as flavonoids and alkaloids, can help to stimulate the immune system and fight off the flu virus. Additionally, yarrow tea can help to alleviate fever, reduce coughing, and soothe a sore throat. Its diaphoretic properties can also promote sweating, which can help to eliminate toxins from the body and speed up recovery. Furthermore, yarrow tea has been used traditionally as a natural remedy for respiratory and digestive issues, making it a valuable tool in managing flu-related symptoms.
remedy_recipe: To make yarrow tea for the flu, start by boiling 2 cups of water and adding 2-3 teaspoons of dried yarrow flowers or 4-6 fresh yarrow flowering tops to a teapot.
Allow the water to steep with the yarrow for 10 minutes, covered, to help extract the medicinal compounds and essential oils.
After 10 minutes, strain the tea to remove the yarrow plant material, leaving only the liquid.
For added flavor and additional flu-fighting properties, consider adding 1 teaspoon of honey and a squeeze of lemon juice to your yarrow tea before drinking.
Sip the yarrow tea slowly, allowing it to warm you from the inside and soothe your sore throat, helping to alleviate symptoms of the flu. Enjoy up to three cups of yarrow tea per day as needed.

remedies.remedy_desc: Hyssop tea helps with flu because it has antiviral and anti-inflammatory properties, which can help reduce symptoms and shorten the duration of the illness. The tea's antioxidants can boost the immune system, helping the body fight off the virus. Additionally, hyssop tea can help soothe a sore throat, relieve congestion, and alleviate coughing fits. Its expectorant properties can help clear mucus and phlegm from the respiratory tract, making it easier to breathe. Furthermore, hyssop tea has been traditionally used to reduce fever and promote sweating, which can help the body eliminate toxins and further support the immune system in combating the flu.
remedy_recipe: To make hyssop tea for flu, start by boiling 1 cup of water in a pot, then add 1-2 teaspoons of dried hyssop leaves or 2-3 teaspoons of fresh hyssop leaves, depending on your preference.
Allow the water to simmer for 10-15 minutes with the hyssop leaves, so the nutrients and flavors can be fully extracted.
After simmering, remove the pot from heat and strain the tea into a cup to remove the leaves.
Add honey or lemon to taste, if desired, for added sweetness and vitamin C.
Sip the tea slowly while it's still warm, and enjoy the soothing effects of hyssop on your throat and respiratory system. Drink 2-3 times a day for best results.

remedies.remedy_desc: Sage tea helps with flu because it has antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory properties that can aid in alleviating symptoms and reducing the duration of the illness. The rosmarinic acid and safficinolide present in sage have been shown to inhibit the growth of viruses, including those that cause the flu. Additionally, sage tea can help soothe a sore throat, reduce fever, and alleviate coughing fits, providing relief from some of the most unpleasant symptoms of the flu. Furthermore, sage is rich in antioxidants, which can help boost the immune system and speed up recovery. Lastly, drinking warm sage tea can promote relaxation and improve sleep, which is essential for the body to fight off the flu virus and recover more quickly.
remedy_recipe: To make sage tea for flu, first bring 1 cup of water to a boil, then add 1-2 teaspoons of fresh sage leaves or 1 teaspoon of dried sage leaves, ensuring the water is covering the herbs.
Allow the mixture to steep for 10-15 minutes, ensuring it's covered to preserve the essential oils from evaporating.
Strain the tea to remove the sage leaves, discarding them once strained.
If desired, add honey or lemon to taste for added soothing properties and flavor.
Sip the warm sage tea, ideally 2-3 times a day, while symptoms persist to potentially alleviate flu symptoms.

remedies.remedy_desc: Marshmallow root tea helps with flu because it has anti-inflammatory and expectorant properties that soothe a sore throat, reduce inflammation, and help expel mucus from the respiratory tract. Its demulcent quality comes from the high mucilage content, which forms a protective layer on the mucous membranes, providing relief from irritation and coughing. Marshmallow root tea also boosts the immune system by stimulating the production of white blood cells, helping the body fight off the flu virus more effectively. Furthermore, it helps reduce fever and alleviates body aches, providing overall comfort during flu recovery. Lastly, marshmallow root tea is a natural diaphoretic, promoting perspiration and helping the body eliminate toxins, which can speed up the healing process.
remedy_recipe: To make marshmallow root tea for the flu, boil 1 quart of water in a pot, then add 2-3 teaspoons of dried marshmallow root and reduce heat to a simmer; allow the mixture to steep, covered, for 15-20 minutes.
After steeping, strain the tea through a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth into a teapot or mug, discarding the used marshmallow root.
For added immune support, consider adding 1-2 teaspoons of dried elderberry or echinacea root to the simmering water along with the marshmallow root.
Optional: sweeten the tea with honey or stevia to taste, and add a slice of lemon or a cinnamon stick for additional flavor and wellness benefits.
Drink the marshmallow root tea 2-3 times a day while symptoms persist, sipping it slowly to soothe a sore throat and support respiratory health during the flu.

remedies.remedy_desc: Holy basil tea helps with flu because it has antiviral and immunomodulatory properties that can help the body fight off the flu virus. The tea's warm temperature can also help soothe a sore throat and alleviate congestion. Additionally, holy basil, also known as tulsi, contains high levels of antioxidants that can help boost the immune system and reduce inflammation. Furthermore, holy basil tea has been used in traditional Ayurvedic medicine for centuries as a natural remedy for various ailments, including the flu. However, it is important to note that while holy basil tea can provide relief of symptoms and support the immune system, it should not be used as a substitute for medical treatment for the flu.
remedy_recipe: In a cup, place one teaspoon of dried holy basil leaves (or two teaspoons of fresh leaves) and add 8 ounces of boiling water.
Cover the cup and let it steep for 5-10 minutes to allow the active compounds to be released into the water.
Strain the tea to remove the leaves, and optionally, add honey or lemon to taste.
Drink the tea while still warm, up to three times a day, as it helps to alleviate flu symptoms and soothe a sore throat.
For optimal results, continue consuming holy basil tea for several days until flu symptoms subside.

remedies.remedy_desc: Rosehip tea helps with flu because it is packed with vitamin C and antioxidants that can strengthen the immune system and help the body fight off the virus. Additionally, it contains anti-inflammatory properties that can alleviate symptoms such as fever, sore throat, and body aches. Furthermore, rosehip tea has been shown to have antiviral and antimicrobial effects, which may help to reduce the duration of the flu. It can also help to soothe a cough and relieve congestion, making it easier to breathe. Drinking rosehip tea regularly can provide a preventative boost to the immune system, reducing the likelihood of getting the flu in the first place.
remedy_recipe: To make rosehip tea for the flu, start by boiling 1 cup of water and adding 2 teaspoons of dried rosehips, then let it simmer for 10-15 minutes.
After simmering, remove the tea from heat and let it steep for an additional 10 minutes to allow the nutrients to fully infuse.
Strain the tea to remove the rosehips, then add honey or lemon to taste, if desired.
For added benefits, consider adding other immune-boosting ingredients such as a slice of ginger or a cinnamon stick during the simmering process.
Drink the tea while still warm, up to three times a day, to help soothe symptoms of the flu and support your immune system.

remedies.remedy_desc: Cloves tea helps with flu because it contains eugenol, a compound with strong antioxidant and antiviral properties that can help fight off the flu virus. Additionally, cloves have been shown to boost the immune system, helping the body to better defend itself against the virus. The warm temperature of the tea can also help to soothe a sore throat and alleviate congestion, while the hydration provided by the tea can help to prevent dehydration, a common symptom of the flu. Furthermore, cloves tea can help to reduce inflammation, which can help to alleviate some of the aches and pains associated with the flu. Finally, cloves tea has been used in traditional medicine for centuries as a remedy for respiratory and digestive ailments, making it a time-tested natural remedy for the flu.
remedy_recipe: In a small saucepan, bring 1 cup of water to a boil, then add 3-5 whole cloves (adjust quantity for stronger flavor if desired), reduce heat and let it simmer for 10 minutes to allow the water to absorb the cloves' medicinal properties.
Turn off the heat, and let the cloves steep for an additional 5-10 minutes to ensure maximum extraction of beneficial compounds; the longer it steeps, the stronger the tea.
Strain the tea into a cup, discarding the cloves, and add honey or lemon to taste, if desired, for added soothing effects and vitamin C.
Sip the tea slowly, allowing it to coat your throat and provide relief from inflammation and congestion; breathe in the aroma for added aromatherapy benefits.
Drink 2-3 cups of cloves tea daily, as needed, during flu symptoms, but discontinue use if any adverse reactions occur.

remedies.remedy_desc: Fenugreek tea helps with flu because it contains antiviral and expectorant properties that assist in alleviating congestion, reducing fever, and soothing a sore throat. Its anti-inflammatory properties help in relieving inflammation in the respiratory tract, making it easier to breathe. Furthermore, fenugreek tea is rich in antioxidants that boost the immune system, helping the body fight off the flu virus. Additionally, it helps in breaking down mucus, making it less sticky and easier to expel, which can speed up recovery. Lastly, drinking warm fenugreek tea can provide soothing relief to a sore throat and alleviate coughing fits, providing much-needed relief to those suffering from the flu.
remedy_recipe: In a teapot, combine 1 teaspoon of dried fenugreek leaves or 2 teaspoons of fenugreek seeds with 4 cups of water; bring the mixture to a boil, then reduce heat and let it simmer for 10 minutes.
Strain the tea to remove the fenugreek leaves or seeds, and pour the liquid into a cup.
Add honey, lemon, or any other desired sweeteners or flavorings to taste; stir well to combine.
Cover the cup and let the tea steep for an additional 5 minutes to allow the flavors to meld together.
Sip the fenugreek tea slowly, enjoying its warm and soothing properties as you nurse your flu symptoms.

remedies.remedy_desc: Horehound tea helps with flu because it contains compounds that act as expectorants, helping to loosen and expel mucus from the respiratory tract, providing relief from congestion and coughing. Additionally, horehound has antiviral and anti-inflammatory properties that can help reduce fever, alleviate inflammation, and potentially inhibit the replication of the flu virus. Furthermore, horehound tea can help soothe a sore throat and alleviate pain, while its diaphoretic properties can promote sweating, which may help the body eliminate toxins and further combat the flu. It is important to note that while horehound tea can provide symptomatic relief, it should not be used as a substitute for medical treatment or antiviral medications prescribed by a healthcare professional for the flu. Lastly, horehound tea should be used with caution, as it can have side effects and interact with certain medications, so it is always best to consult with a healthcare provider before using it as a flu remedy.
remedy_recipe: To make horehound tea for flu, start by boiling 2 cups of water in a pot, then add 2 tablespoons of dried horehound leaves or 4 tablespoons of fresh horehound leaves.
Allow the mixture to steep for 10-15 minutes, covered, to extract the medicinal properties of the horehound.
Strain the tea to remove the leaves, and squeeze in the juice of half a lemon for added vitamin C and to balance the bitter taste of horehound.
Add honey to taste, as it not only sweetens the tea but also has soothing effects on a sore throat.
Drink the tea while still warm, up to three times a day, to alleviate flu symptoms such as congestion, cough, and sore throat.

remedies.remedy_desc: Oregano tea helps with flu because it contains carvacrol and rosmarnic acid, both of which have anti-viral and anti-inflammatory properties that can help reduce symptoms and duration of the flu. Additionally, oregano has been shown to stimulate the production of white blood cells, boosting the immune system's ability to fight off the virus. The warm temperature of the tea can also help soothe a sore throat and alleviate congestion. Furthermore, oregano tea is a natural and caffeine-free alternative to over-the-counter flu medications, making it a suitable option for those who prefer a more holistic approach to treating the flu. However, it is important to note that while oregano tea can help alleviate flu symptoms, it should not be used as a substitute for medical treatment, especially for severe or persistent symptoms.
remedy_recipe: In a teapot, combine 1 cup of water and 1 teaspoon of dried oregano leaves, then bring the water to a boil on the stove.
Once boiling, reduce heat and let the oregano simmer in the hot water for 5-10 minutes.
Strain the tea into a mug, discarding the oregano leaves, then add honey or lemon to taste if desired.
For added flu-fighting power, squeeze half a lemon and stir in 1 tablespoon of honey into the tea.
Sip the hot oregano tea slowly, allowing it to soothe a sore throat and alleviate congestion.

remedies.remedy_desc: Thyme tea helps with flu because it has antimicrobial and antioxidant properties that can strengthen the immune system and help the body fight off the virus. The herb's active compounds, such as thymol and carvacrol, have been shown to have antiviral effects, which can help reduce the severity and duration of flu symptoms. Thyme tea can also help soothe a sore throat, relieve congestion, and alleviate coughing fits, providing much-needed relief for those suffering from the flu. Furthermore, thyme tea's warm temperature can help break up mucus and promote sweating, which can help the body eliminate toxins and speed up recovery. Finally, drinking thyme tea can help prevent dehydration, which is a common issue for those with the flu, by keeping the body hydrated and maintaining electrolyte balance.
remedy_recipe: In a small pot, bring 1 cup of water to a boil, then add 1 teaspoon of dried thyme leaves, reduce heat and let it simmer for 5-7 minutes to allow the active compounds to be extracted into the water.
Turn off the heat, cover the pot and let it steep for an additional 10 minutes to ensure a strong infusion.
Strain the tea to remove the spent thyme leaves, and discard them.
Add honey or lemon to taste, if desired, for added soothing properties and to improve the flavor.
Sip the tea slowly, allowing it to coat your throat and soothe your respiratory system, and repeat as needed throughout the day.

remedies.remedy_desc: Boneset tea helps with flu because it contains properties that can alleviate symptoms and potentially reduce the duration of the illness. The herbal tea is known for its anti-inflammatory, antipyretic, and immune-stimulating effects, which can help the body fight off the flu virus. Boneset tea can soothe aches, pains, and fever, providing relief to those suffering from the flu. Additionally, it may help clear respiratory congestion and alleviate coughing, making it easier to breathe. However, it is essential to note that while boneset tea can provide symptomatic relief, it should not be used as a substitute for medical treatment, especially for severe or persistent flu cases.
remedy_recipe: To make boneset tea for the flu, start by boiling 2 cups of water in a pot, then add 2-3 teaspoons of dried boneset herb (Eupatorium perfoliatum) and let it simmer, covered, for 10-15 minutes.
Strain the tea to remove the herb, then stir in honey or lemon juice to taste, if desired, for added flavor and vitamin C.
Drink the boneset tea while it's still warm, up to three times a day, as needed to help alleviate flu symptoms.
For best results, continue drinking boneset tea for several days, as it may take a few days for the herb's antiviral and anti-inflammatory properties to take full effect.
Remember to consult with a healthcare provider before using boneset tea as a treatment for the flu, especially for children, pregnant women, and people with medical conditions or taking medications.

remedies.remedy_desc: Astragalus tea helps with flu because it contains several beneficial compounds, including saponins, flavonoids, and polysaccharides, which have been shown to boost the immune system and help the body fight off viruses. Additionally, astragalus has anti-inflammatory properties, which can help reduce symptoms such as fever, sore throat, and muscle aches. Furthermore, studies have suggested that astragalus may have antiviral effects, which could help prevent the flu virus from replicating in the body. Drinking astragalus tea can also help keep the body hydrated, which is important when fighting off an illness. However, it's important to note that astragalus tea should not be used as a substitute for medical treatment for the flu and should be used as a complementary therapy.
remedy_recipe: To make astragalus tea for the flu, start by bringing 4 cups of water to a boil in a saucepan, then add 2 tablespoons of dried astragalus root slices, reduce heat to low, and simmer for 30 minutes.
After 30 minutes, remove the saucepan from heat and strain the tea into a teapot or heat-resistant container, discarding the spent astragalus root slices.
Optional: for added flavor and potential additional cold-fighting benefits, consider adding 1-2 slices of fresh ginger and/or a cinnamon stick to the simmering water in step 1.
To serve, pour the hot astragalus tea into cups, adding honey or lemon to taste if desired.
Sip the tea slowly, ideally 2-3 times a day, while resting and allowing the tea's immune-boosting properties to help fight off the flu.

intro: Herbal teas can help with flu by providing hydration and soothing a sore throat. Some herbs, like chamomile and ginger, have anti-inflammatory properties that can help reduce inflammation in the throat and nasal passages. Additionally, some herbal teas contain vitamins and antioxidants that can help boost the immune system and fight off the virus. Furthermore, the warm temperature of the tea can help alleviate congestion and break up mucus. Lastly, drinking herbal tea can promote relaxation and help individuals rest, which is crucial for recovery from the flu.
